title: Customs Clearance I Flashcards
description: 295 flashcards covering all 12 topics of Customs Clearance I based on EACCMA 2004.

flashcards.front: What are prohibited goods?
flashcards.back: Goods that are not allowed to be imported, exported, or transferred into or outside Partner States.

flashcards.front: How does EACCMA define prohibited goods?
flashcards.back: Goods whose importation, exportation, or carriage coastwise is prohibited under the Act or any law in force in the Partner States.

flashcards.front: What are restricted goods?
flashcards.back: Goods whose importation or exportation is allowed only after specific conditions are met.

flashcards.front: What is the main difference between prohibited and restricted goods?
flashcards.back: Prohibited goods are not allowed at all; restricted goods are allowed only with permits, certificates, or approvals.

flashcards.front: Name four examples of prohibited goods.
flashcards.back: Counterfeit currency, pornographic materials, matches made with white phosphorus, narcotic drugs under international control.

flashcards.front: Which kind of false money is prohibited?
flashcards.back: Counterfeit currency notes and coins, and money not of the established standard in weight or fineness.

flashcards.front: Why are pornographic and obscene materials prohibited?
flashcards.back: Mainly for social and moral control.

flashcards.front: Why are narcotic drugs prohibited?
flashcards.back: For health, safety, and international control reasons.

flashcards.front: Why are hazardous wastes prohibited?
flashcards.back: For environmental and health protection.

flashcards.front: Give examples of restricted goods.
flashcards.back: Postal franking machines, traps for game animals, unwrought precious metals, arms and ammunition, worked ivory, GM products, second-hand tyres.

flashcards.front: Why are arms and ammunition restricted?
flashcards.back: Because of security and safety concerns.

flashcards.front: Why are endangered flora and fauna products restricted?
flashcards.back: To comply with CITES and protect biodiversity.

flashcards.front: Why are genetically modified products restricted?
flashcards.back: Because they require regulatory control and safety assessment.

flashcards.front: Why are second-hand tyres restricted?
flashcards.back: For safety, environmental, and quality-control reasons.

flashcards.front: Which schedule deals with prohibited and restricted exports?
flashcards.back: The Third Schedule.

flashcards.front: What does Section 70 of EACCMA say about prohibited exports?
flashcards.back: Goods specified in Part A of the Third Schedule are prohibited goods, and their exportation is prohibited.

flashcards.front: Give two examples of prohibited exports from the notes.
flashcards.back: Used or scrap cables and conductors of aluminium, steel, and copper.

flashcards.front: What are restricted exports?
flashcards.back: Goods in Part B of the Third Schedule whose export is allowed only if conditions regulating their export are met.

flashcards.front: Give five examples of restricted exports.
flashcards.back: Waste and scrap of ferrous cast iron, timber, fresh unprocessed Nile perch and tilapia, wood charcoal, used automobile batteries and lead scrap.

flashcards.front: What vessel-size rule applies to some restricted exports?
flashcards.back: Warehoused goods, goods under duty drawback, and transshipped goods shall not be exported in vessels of less than 250 tons register.

flashcards.front: What document support must exist before restricted goods are cleared?
flashcards.back: Import permits, certificates, or authority from the relevant agency.

flashcards.front: Who controls restricted goods besides Customs?
flashcards.back: Specific government agencies depending on the nature of the goods.

flashcards.front: Name six reasons goods may be prohibited or restricted.
flashcards.back: Political, health, economic, safety and security, environmental and agricultural, and social reasons.

flashcards.front: How should prohibited imports generally be handled?
flashcards.back: They should not be cleared for import; they are liable to seizure and other enforcement action.

flashcards.front: How should restricted imports generally be handled?
flashcards.back: They may be cleared only after the importer satisfies all permit and agency requirements.

flashcards.front: How should prohibited exports be handled?
flashcards.back: Exportation should be stopped because the law prohibits it.

flashcards.front: How should restricted exports be handled?
flashcards.back: Export is allowed only if conditions and authorizations are satisfied.

flashcards.front: Exam trap: Are all dangerous goods prohibited?
flashcards.back: No. Some are prohibited, while others are only restricted and may move with approval.

flashcards.front: What are the four import categories in the notes?
flashcards.back: Direct imports, temporary imports, re-imports, and warehoused goods.

flashcards.front: What is an approved place of loading/unloading?
flashcards.back: A quay, jetty, wharf, or other place, including part of a Customs airport, appointed by the Commissioner for loading or unloading goods.

flashcards.front: What is a boarding station?
flashcards.back: A place appointed by the Commissioner for aircraft or vessels to bring to for boarding or disembarkation of officers.

flashcards.front: What is cargo?
flashcards.back: All goods imported or exported in an aircraft, vehicle, or vessel, excluding stores for use by the craft, crew and passengers, and bona fide personal baggage.

flashcards.front: What is a Customs area?
flashcards.back: A place appointed under section 12 for Customs operations, including deposit of goods under Customs control.

flashcards.front: What is a Customs warehouse?
flashcards.back: A place approved for deposit of unentered, unexamined, abandoned, detained, or seized goods for security of goods or duties.

flashcards.front: Who is a master?
flashcards.back: Any person having or taking charge or command of an aircraft or vessel.

flashcards.front: What does 'import' mean?
flashcards.back: To bring or cause to be brought into the Partner States from a foreign country.

flashcards.front: What is a port under Customs law?
flashcards.back: A place appointed by the Council for purposes of Customs law; for aircraft, a port means a Customs airport.

flashcards.front: Who is a proper officer?
flashcards.back: Any officer whose right or duty it is to require or perform acts referred to in the Act.

flashcards.front: What is a sufferance wharf?
flashcards.back: A place other than an approved loading or unloading place where the Commissioner may allow goods to be loaded or unloaded.

flashcards.front: What is a transit shed?
flashcards.back: A building or premises appointed by the Commissioner in writing for deposit of goods under Customs control.

flashcards.front: What are uncustomed goods?
flashcards.back: Dutiable goods whose full duty has not been paid, and any goods dealt with contrary to Customs laws whether dutiable or not.

flashcards.front: What are the three main modes of cargo arrival mentioned?
flashcards.back: Overland, by air, and by sea.

flashcards.front: How may cargo arrive overland according to the notes?
flashcards.back: By motor vehicle, train, pipeline, animal, and cables.

flashcards.front: What does Section 29 require of a person in charge of a vehicle arriving overland?
flashcards.back: Report arrival, furnish information, declare truth of particulars, answer questions, produce documents, and make due entry.

flashcards.front: To whom must the driver report arrival?
flashcards.back: The officer stationed at the frontier port where the vehicle entered the Partner State.

flashcards.front: What declaration form is mentioned for overland vehicle arrival?
flashcards.back: Form C17.

flashcards.front: What documents may the proper officer demand from the driver?
flashcards.back: Consignment notes and other relevant documents.

flashcards.front: What happens if a person conveys goods by other means, not vehicle or train?
flashcards.back: Under Section 31, that person must still fulfill reporting, documentation, questioning, and declaration duties similar to Section 29.

flashcards.front: How is wet cargo arriving overland treated?
flashcards.back: Section 32 applies; the pipeline operator must report and record the nature and quantities and provide apparatus for proper records.

flashcards.front: Give examples of wet cargo.
flashcards.back: Petroleum and gas.

flashcards.front: What is the Customs area for cargo arriving on an international train?
flashcards.back: The border railway station.

flashcards.front: Who acts on behalf of the railway company at the station?
flashcards.back: The station master or other person in charge.

flashcards.front: What must the station master submit on arrival of the train?
flashcards.back: Copies of invoices, consignment notes, waybills, and other documents relating to the goods in wagons.

flashcards.front: Can the station master allow goods to leave the Customs area without permission?
flashcards.back: No. That is an offence.

flashcards.front: Can goods required to be entered at another station be delivered without written permission?
flashcards.back: No.

flashcards.front: Through where must a person in charge bring goods into a Partner State?
flashcards.back: Through a port appointed under Section 11, unless otherwise permitted.

flashcards.front: What is rummaging?
flashcards.back: An intense search of a vessel or aircraft by the proper officer, usually on a risk basis.

flashcards.front: Which section gives the proper officer power to search aircraft or vessels?
flashcards.back: Section 246 of EACCMA.

flashcards.front: What does Regulation 25 require during rummaging?
flashcards.back: The crew must provide all necessary assistance to officers conducting the search.

flashcards.front: Who must be informed first when a rummage squad boards a vessel?
flashcards.back: The Master, or in his absence the Chief Officer.

flashcards.front: What should the officer in charge request from the Master?
flashcards.back: A ship's officer to accompany the squad and crew assistance if dismantling is needed.

flashcards.front: What is checked against Form C.9 during rummage?
flashcards.back: Crew declarations against goods held in their quarters.

flashcards.front: Which part of a vessel should be rummaged first?
flashcards.back: The crew's quarters.

flashcards.front: Why are crew quarters searched first?
flashcards.back: To check undeclared dutiable goods while officers are still clean enough to handle personal effects.

flashcards.front: Can living quarters be rummaged in the absence of occupants?
flashcards.back: No. At least one occupant must be present.

flashcards.front: When may personal effects and lockers be searched?
flashcards.back: Only in the presence of the owner.

flashcards.front: What caution applies when searching passenger accommodation and public rooms?
flashcards.back: Do not disturb passengers or damage fittings and furnishings.

flashcards.front: What caution applies when searching machinery and structural fittings?
flashcards.back: Do not endanger safety or mechanical operation; consult a competent ship's officer.

flashcards.front: What should officers do if undeclared goods are found during rummage?
flashcards.back: Note the concealment, seize the goods, report to the officer in charge, show them to the Master, and try to trace the owner.

flashcards.front: What happens to seized goods after rummage?
flashcards.back: They are marked for identification and taken ashore to the Customs warehouse.

flashcards.front: What must be issued or prepared after seizure?
flashcards.back: A report, and possibly a Notice of Seizure and offence report.

flashcards.front: Why are coastal vessels occasionally rummaged?
flashcards.back: As a revenue precaution against hoarding, illicit transfers, smuggling, and evasion of prohibitions and restrictions.

flashcards.front: Are rummages of all scheduled foreign flights mandatory?
flashcards.back: No. Scheduled services should be rummaged minimally consistent with revenue control.

flashcards.front: Which aircraft rummages are mandatory?
flashcards.back: Non-scheduled, charter, and private aircraft arriving from foreign.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of rummaging an aircraft?
flashcards.back: To ensure all goods, baggage, stores, and cargo are lawfully on board and properly accounted for.

flashcards.front: What happens to goods on an aircraft not accounted for to the officer's satisfaction?
flashcards.back: They must be detained and the facts reported.

flashcards.front: When may a person be searched?
flashcards.back: When an officer has reasonable grounds to believe the person has uncustomed goods.

flashcards.front: What if the person objects to being searched?
flashcards.back: The person must be taken before the station manager or senior officer to decide whether the search should proceed.

flashcards.front: Where should searches of persons take place?
flashcards.back: In a cabin, Customs office, or other private place.

flashcards.front: Who may search a female suspect?
flashcards.back: Only a female officer.

flashcards.front: What must the master or agent produce on arrival?
flashcards.back: All books and documents relating to the craft, cargo, stores, baggage, crew, and passengers as required by the proper officer.

flashcards.front: What is Form C2?
flashcards.back: Inward report for a vessel.

flashcards.front: What is Form C4?
flashcards.back: Inward report for an aircraft; it is also mentioned as the clearance form for an aircraft.

flashcards.front: What information is contained in the inward report?
flashcards.back: Particulars such as nationality, tonnage, crew numbers, and port of landing.

flashcards.front: What is Form C3?
flashcards.back: Parcels list showing packages or parcels imported for which no bill of lading was issued.

flashcards.front: What is included in the crew list?
flashcards.back: Full name, gender, nationality, and details of the master and crew members.

flashcards.front: What is Form C5?
flashcards.back: Passenger list showing passengers disembarking and those remaining on board.

flashcards.front: What is Form C6?
flashcards.back: Declaration Advice Consumable Stores, giving details of all dutiable stores on board.

flashcards.front: What is Form C7?
flashcards.back: Crew Declaration of all dutiable goods in possession of each crew member.

flashcards.front: What is the last port of call list?
flashcards.back: A list of the vessel's last ten port calls in chronological order.

flashcards.front: What does the arms and ammunition list show?
flashcards.back: Details of all arms and ammunition on board.

flashcards.front: What does the narcotics list show?
flashcards.back: Details of all narcotics on board.

flashcards.front: When should the cargo manifest be submitted?
flashcards.back: Before arrival of the vessel or aircraft, under Section 24.

flashcards.front: What does Regulation 23 say about dutiable stores?
flashcards.back: Stores for crew and passengers may be produced separately and approved or placed under seal by the proper officer.

flashcards.front: What are the duty-free allowable stores per crew member?
flashcards.back: 200 cigarettes or 200g tobacco, 750 ml potable spirits, and 3 litres of wine or beer.

flashcards.front: What happens to quantities above the allowable stores?
flashcards.back: They are sealed by the proper officer.

flashcards.front: Is breaking the seal without approval an offence?
flashcards.back: Yes.

flashcards.front: What is issued once the proper officer is satisfied all requirements are met?
flashcards.back: A certificate of clearance.

flashcards.front: What fee is mentioned for certificate of clearance?
flashcards.back: USD 3.

flashcards.front: What can the proper officer permit to be amended in an inward report?
flashcards.back: Destination, ownership, status of goods, or obvious errors/omissions.

flashcards.front: What documents support amendment of a manifest?
flashcards.back: A manifest corrector and an affidavit.

flashcards.front: What fee is paid for amendment of inward report?
flashcards.back: USD 10.

flashcards.front: What is short landed cargo?
flashcards.back: Cargo that was manifested but is not physically on board for unloading.

flashcards.front: What is excess landed cargo?
flashcards.back: Cargo not manifested but physically on board for unloading.

flashcards.front: Name three reasons that may justify short landed cargo.
flashcards.back: Goods were not shipped, were discharged at a previous port, were overcarried to a later port.

flashcards.front: Give four more possible explanations for short landed cargo.
flashcards.back: Returned after overcarriage, carried by another craft, lost at sea, stolen or destroyed before arrival within EAC ports.

flashcards.front: How is excess landed cargo regularized?
flashcards.back: By a supplementary manifest.

flashcards.front: What is the fee for a supplementary manifest?
flashcards.back: USD 10.

flashcards.front: What does 'carried coastwise' mean?
flashcards.back: Goods conveyed by sea or air from one part of a Partner State to another part thereof.

flashcards.front: What is a coasting vessel or aircraft?
flashcards.back: A vessel or aircraft carrying goods coastwise within the Partner States.

flashcards.front: Which provisions govern coasting vessels and aircraft?
flashcards.back: Sections 96–109 of EACCMA and Regulations 123–130 of EACCMR.

flashcards.front: What document must the master present for coastwise carriage or transfer?
flashcards.back: A transire.

flashcards.front: What does the transire do?
flashcards.back: It authorizes carriage of the goods and also serves as certificate of clearance for the coastwise voyage.

flashcards.front: In what form must the account for coastwise cargo be delivered?
flashcards.back: In triplicate on the prescribed form.

flashcards.front: Where must coastwise goods be loaded or unloaded?
flashcards.back: At an approved place of loading or a sufferance wharf.

flashcards.front: What may the proper officer require after unloading coastwise goods?
flashcards.back: That goods be conveyed immediately to a Customs area or transit shed.

flashcards.front: What may the proper officer require for goods transferred by road?
flashcards.back: Immediate conveyance to a Customs house or another directed place.

flashcards.front: What happens if goods reported to be unloaded are in fact not unloaded?
flashcards.back: The master or agent must pay duty unless acceptable explanation is given.

flashcards.front: What must a master do if a vessel or aircraft is accidentally forced to land in EAC States?
flashcards.back: Immediately report the cargo and stores to the nearest Customs or administrative officer.

flashcards.front: What happens to cargo and stores of an abandoned vessel or aircraft?
flashcards.back: They are liable to seizure unless satisfactory explanation is given.

flashcards.front: In case of loss or wreck, what must the master or agent do?
flashcards.back: Report the aircraft or vessel and its cargo and stores with all reasonable speed to the nearest officer.

flashcards.front: When are goods deemed to be entered under Section 2(2)(a)?
flashcards.back: When entry is made and lodged properly and duty or deposit is paid, or security is given.

flashcards.front: When are goods deemed to be entered for home consumption?
flashcards.back: When declared for use in a Partner State other than temporary use and requirements of entry are fulfilled.

flashcards.front: Within how many days must goods be entered?
flashcards.back: Within 21 days after commencement of discharge, or on arrival for vehicles, unless further time is allowed.

flashcards.front: What happens if cargo remains unentered after 21 days?
flashcards.back: It becomes liable to Customs warehouse rent.

flashcards.front: What if cargo is entered but not removed from the port within 21 days?
flashcards.back: It is deemed to be in a Customs warehouse.

flashcards.front: Can goods be entered before arrival?
flashcards.back: Yes, under Section 34(3), allowing pre-arrival processing.

flashcards.front: Why is pre-arrival processing important?
flashcards.back: It reduces clearance time and costs.

flashcards.front: Who bears the burden of proving correctness of entry particulars?
flashcards.back: The owner, not Customs.

flashcards.front: For what purposes may cargo be entered?
flashcards.back: Home consumption, warehousing, transhipment, transit, or export processing zones.

flashcards.front: Name five basic supporting documents for import entry.
flashcards.back: Bill of lading/airway bill/road manifest, packing list, commercial invoice, freight invoice, insurance debit note.

flashcards.front: Name five more supporting documents.
flashcards.back: Certificate of export, certificate of conformity, registration/incorporation/ID, TIN, certificate of origin.

flashcards.front: Name five additional possible supporting documents.
flashcards.back: Import Declaration Form, motor vehicle logbook, certificate of roadworthiness, permits/licenses, chemical analysis report.

flashcards.front: What proof of payment may support entry?
flashcards.back: Telegraphic transfer, letter of credit, and similar evidence.

flashcards.front: What is Form C36?
flashcards.back: Declaration of Value, a statement by the importer about trading terms with the foreign supplier.

flashcards.front: Who must complete Form C36?
flashcards.back: The owner of the goods, not the Customs agent.

flashcards.front: Who completes Certificate A on Form C36?
flashcards.back: An importer not related to the supplier.

flashcards.front: Who completes Certificate B on Form C36?
flashcards.back: An importer related to the supplier.

flashcards.front: What is a provisional entry?
flashcards.back: An entry lodged when there are insufficient documents or information.

flashcards.front: What security may the Commissioner require for provisional entry?
flashcards.back: Cash deposit, bank guarantee, or bond.

flashcards.front: Within how long must a provisional entry be perfected?
flashcards.back: Within three months, or further period allowed.

flashcards.front: What are the uses of a Customs declaration?
flashcards.back: Tax assessment, tax payment arrangement, determination of bond in force, document processing across institutions, and obtaining genuine clearance.

flashcards.front: Why must Customs declarations be standardized internationally?
flashcards.back: To facilitate trade and ensure uniform minimum data requirements.

flashcards.front: Which international instrument influences declaration layout?
flashcards.back: The Revised Kyoto Convention through the UN layout key.

flashcards.front: What did the UN layout key lead to?
flashcards.back: Development of the Single Administrative Document (SAD).

flashcards.front: Name goods that may be cleared without an entry.
flashcards.back: Mail bags and postal articles, bona fide passenger baggage, human remains, diplomatic bags.

flashcards.front: Which sensitive goods may be cleared without entry subject to an undertaking?
flashcards.back: Bullion, currency notes, coin, and perishable goods.

flashcards.front: Within how long must the owner furnish the necessary entry for those exceptions?
flashcards.back: Within 48 hours of delivery.

flashcards.front: How are goods in a transit shed legally treated?
flashcards.back: They are deemed to be in the importing vessel or aircraft until delivered.

flashcards.front: Who remains responsible for goods in a transit shed?
flashcards.back: The owner or agent.

flashcards.front: What if reported goods are not duly unloaded and deposited in a transit shed or Customs area?
flashcards.back: The master or agent must pay duty unless he explains satisfactorily.

flashcards.front: Who commits an offence if goods in custody are not accounted for?
flashcards.back: The owner of vessel/aircraft, his agent, or the transit shed owner.

flashcards.front: Who is liable for reshipment or destruction costs of condemned goods?
flashcards.back: The owner of vessel or aircraft, his agent, or the transit shed owner.

flashcards.front: Name the main import Customs regimes in the notes.
flashcards.back: Imports for home use, temporary imports, re-imports, and warehousing.

flashcards.front: Give examples under imports for home use.
flashcards.back: Duty paid, provisional import declaration, release of perishables, tax free by tariff.

flashcards.front: Give examples of ex-warehouse home use entries.
flashcards.back: Duty paid, duty paid for regional market, tax free, duty exempt under Third Schedule.

flashcards.front: Give examples of home use after temporary importation.
flashcards.back: Home use after temporary importation in unaltered state, and after temporary importation for repair.

flashcards.front: What is EX-EPZ home use?
flashcards.back: Home use entry for goods moving from an Export Processing Zone into home consumption.

flashcards.front: What are the two temporary import regimes listed?
flashcards.back: Temporary importation for return in unaltered state, and temporary importation for repair.

flashcards.front: Give examples of re-import regimes.
flashcards.back: Re-import after temporary export for repair with charge, without charge, after substantial change, and rejected/returned exports.

flashcards.front: Give examples of warehousing regimes.
flashcards.back: Warehousing of goods, warehousing for regional market, warehousing for crude petroleum products, ex-warehouse removal.

flashcards.front: Give examples involving MUB and EPZ.
flashcards.back: Entry into MUB/EPZ from foreign, entry of home-produced goods into MUB/EPZ, and transfer between MUBs or EPZs.

flashcards.front: What is Form C17 mainly used for in the notes?
flashcards.back: Customs declaration/entry for goods and for overland arrival declaration.

flashcards.front: Name five data items on Form C17.
flashcards.back: Importer details, exporter details, declarant details, processing office, manifest number.

flashcards.front: Name five more data items on Form C17.
flashcards.back: Date of arrival, agent details, mode of transport, nationality of transport, marks and numbers.

flashcards.front: Name five more data items on Form C17.
flashcards.back: Description of goods, Customs value, total gross weight, net weight, unit of quantity.

flashcards.front: Name four more data items on Form C17.
flashcards.back: Country of origin, rate of duty, duty payable, other charges, grand total payable.

flashcards.front: What are PGAs?
flashcards.back: Partner Government Agencies involved in clearance of goods; previously called OGAs.

flashcards.front: What broad roles do PGAs play?
flashcards.back: Regulatory, security, and tax collection roles.

flashcards.front: Name six PGAs from the notes.
flashcards.back: KEBS, KEPHIS, KPA, NIS, KPS, Immigration Department.

flashcards.front: Name six more PGAs from the notes.
flashcards.back: Port Health, Kenya Radiation Authority, KMA, NBA, KWS, ACA.

flashcards.front: Why are PGAs important in clearance?
flashcards.back: Customs works with them to ensure compliance, regulation, security, and specialized approvals.

flashcards.front: Which PGA would most likely handle standards compliance?
flashcards.back: KEBS.

flashcards.front: Which PGA would most likely handle plant-health matters?
flashcards.back: KEPHIS.

flashcards.front: Which PGA would most likely handle wildlife-related goods?
flashcards.back: KWS.

flashcards.front: Which PGA would most likely handle biosafety or GM products?
flashcards.back: NBA.

flashcards.front: According to Article 39 of the Customs Union Protocol, what are sources of Customs law?
flashcards.back: Treaty provisions, Customs Union Protocol and annexes, Council regulations/directives, court decisions, Community Acts, and relevant principles of international law.

flashcards.front: What is a common market in simple terms?
flashcards.back: A group of countries working together mainly to promote trade and common interests.

flashcards.front: Which countries formed the EAC in 1967?
flashcards.back: Kenya, Uganda, and Tanzania.

flashcards.front: Name the presidents associated with the 1967 EAC formation.
flashcards.back: Jomo Kenyatta, Julius Nyerere, and Milton Obote.

flashcards.front: Give four aims of the original EAC.
flashcards.back: Promote trade, promote unity/cooperation, create a common market, allow free movement of goods and services.

flashcards.front: Why did the EAC collapse in 1977?
flashcards.back: Political differences, lack of common currency, shortage of funds, misunderstandings among presidents, and differing ideologies.

flashcards.front: When was the EAC revived according to the notes?
flashcards.back: 4 March 1996.

flashcards.front: Which heads of state revived the EAC?
flashcards.back: Yoweri Museveni, Daniel arap Moi, and Benjamin Mkapa.

flashcards.front: When was the EAC Treaty signed and when did it enter into force?
flashcards.back: Signed on 30 November 1999 and came into force on 7 July 2000.

flashcards.front: When was the Customs Union Protocol signed?
flashcards.back: 2 March 2004 in Arusha, Tanzania.

flashcards.front: What does the EAC Customs Union provide for?
flashcards.back: Elimination of Customs duties among Partner States, removal of non-tariff barriers, and a common external tariff for imports from foreign countries.

flashcards.front: Which countries joined later and when?
flashcards.back: Rwanda and Burundi joined in 2008 and began applying instruments in July 2009; South Sudan joined in 2016.

flashcards.front: What is the role of Council regulations and directives?
flashcards.back: They are binding on Partner States and Community organs within their jurisdictions.

flashcards.front: What is EACCMA, 2004?
flashcards.back: The Act providing for management and administration of Customs and related matters.

flashcards.front: What is the structure of EACCMA, 2004 according to the notes?
flashcards.back: 21 parts, 253 sections, and 6 schedules.

flashcards.front: What is EACCMR, 2010?
flashcards.back: Regulations made under Section 251 to give effect to EACCMA and govern Customs business.

flashcards.front: What is covered by the Compliance and Enforcement Regulations, 2012?
flashcards.back: Customs administration, cross-border cooperation, post-clearance audit, AEOs, and information management.

flashcards.front: Why must freight logistics practitioners know other national laws too?
flashcards.back: Because company registration, licensing, standards, phytosanitary, pharmaceutical, and poisons laws also affect operations.

flashcards.front: Name the main organs of the EAC listed in the notes.
flashcards.back: Summit, Council, Coordination Committee, Sectoral Committees, East African Court of Justice, East African Legislative Assembly, and Secretariat.

flashcards.front: What is the Summit?
flashcards.back: The Heads of State who provide general direction and impetus.

flashcards.front: What is the Council?
flashcards.back: The policy organ made up of ministers responsible for EAC affairs and Attorneys General.

flashcards.front: What does the Coordination Committee do?
flashcards.back: Submits reports to Council, implements decisions, and coordinates Sectoral Committees.

flashcards.front: What do Sectoral Committees do?
flashcards.back: Prepare implementation programs, monitor implementation, and recommend action.

flashcards.front: What is the East African Court of Justice?
flashcards.back: The judicial body ensuring adherence to law in implementing the Treaty.

flashcards.front: What is the East African Legislative Assembly?
flashcards.back: The legislative organ of the Community.

flashcards.front: What is the Secretariat?
flashcards.back: The executive organ of the Community.

flashcards.front: What are Customs instruments?
flashcards.back: Conventions and agreements related to Customs clearance of goods.

flashcards.front: Name the three instruments emphasized in the notes.
flashcards.back: Revised Kyoto Convention, WTO Trade Facilitation Agreement, and WCO SAFE Framework.

flashcards.front: What is the Revised Kyoto Convention?
flashcards.back: An international agreement providing modern Customs procedures to facilitate legitimate trade while maintaining control.

flashcards.front: Give four key principles of the RKC.
flashcards.back: Transparency and predictability, standardization and simplification, simplified procedures for authorized persons, maximum use of IT.

flashcards.front: Give four benefits of the RKC to Partner States' economies.
flashcards.back: Reduced transaction costs, increased competitiveness, promotion of FDI and industry, increased SME participation.

flashcards.front: Give three benefits of the RKC to the trade community.
flashcards.back: Faster and predictable clearance, transparent procedures and appeals, greater facilitation for compliant traders.

flashcards.front: Give three benefits of the RKC to Customs administrations.
flashcards.back: Enhanced controls, increased trade facilitation, better resource deployment.

flashcards.front: What is the WTO TFA?
flashcards.back: A WTO agreement aimed at making trade flows smoother, freer, and more predictable.

flashcards.front: When did the WTO TFA enter into force?
flashcards.back: 22 February 2017.

flashcards.front: What impacts of the TFA are mentioned for Partner States?
flashcards.back: Reform of national procedures, less red tape, and added export potential.

flashcards.front: What is the SAFE Framework?
flashcards.back: A WCO framework to secure and facilitate global trade and improve supply-chain security.

flashcards.front: Give four aims of the SAFE Framework.
flashcards.back: Global security standards, harmonized supply chain management, enhanced Customs capability, better detection of high-risk consignments.

flashcards.front: What cooperation does SAFE promote between Customs administrations?
flashcards.back: Information exchange, mutual recognition of controls, recognition of AEOs, and administrative assistance.

flashcards.front: What cooperation does SAFE promote beyond Customs?
flashcards.back: Cooperation with other government agencies and with business, including Single Window systems.

flashcards.front: Give two benefits of SAFE to governments.
flashcards.back: Secure trade against terrorism/transnational crime and improve economic growth/development.

flashcards.front: Give two benefits of SAFE to Customs authorities.
flashcards.back: Better risk management and better allocation of resources.

flashcards.front: Give three benefits of SAFE to business.
flashcards.back: Faster processing for AEOs, uniform standards, and reduced multiple reporting requirements.

flashcards.front: What is Customs value in the basic import tax example?
flashcards.back: CIF value in local currency.

flashcards.front: What does CIF stand for?
flashcards.back: Cost, Insurance, and Freight.

flashcards.front: What is the formula for CIF in local currency?
flashcards.back: (FOB + Insurance + Freight) × Exchange rate.

flashcards.front: In the whiskey example, what are the FOB, insurance, and freight values?
flashcards.back: FOB USD 5000, insurance USD 200, freight USD 900.

flashcards.front: In the whiskey example, what is the CIF in USD?
In the whiskey example, what is the CIF in Ksh at 130?
How is import duty computed in the example?
flashcards.back: 25% × Customs value.

flashcards.front: What is the import duty in the whiskey example?
How is excise duty computed in the example?
flashcards.back: (CIF + Import Duty) × Excise rate.

flashcards.front: What is the excise duty in the whiskey example?
flashcards.back: About Ksh 644,313.

flashcards.front: How is VAT computed in the example?
flashcards.back: (CIF + Import Duty + Excise Duty) × VAT rate.

flashcards.front: What is the VAT in the whiskey example?
flashcards.back: About Ksh 261,690.

flashcards.front: What is the total tax payable in the whiskey example?
In what order are taxes normally built on imports?
flashcards.back: Customs value first, then import duty, then excise, then VAT.

flashcards.front: Why is tax on multiple-item consignments tricky?
flashcards.back: Because freight and insurance may be shared across items.

flashcards.front: What is the factor method?
flashcards.back: A method of allocating total CIF to each item using total CIF divided by total FOB.

flashcards.front: What is the factor formula?
flashcards.back: Factor = Total CIF in Ksh / Total FOB in USD.

flashcards.front: In the hammers and helmets example, what is total CIF in Ksh?
In that example, what is the factor?
How is Customs value per item obtained using the factor?
flashcards.back: FOB of each item × factor.

flashcards.front: What are the Customs values of hammers and helmets in the example?
flashcards.back: Hammers Ksh 150,000; helmets Ksh 120,000.

flashcards.front: What extra levies, besides duty and VAT, appear in the multiple-item example?
flashcards.back: RDL and IDF.

flashcards.front: What does CRSP stand for?
flashcards.back: Current Retail Selling Price.

flashcards.front: Why do some Customs administrations use CRSP for used vehicles?
flashcards.back: To levy duties in a transparent, fair, and predictable manner despite pricing variations in used cars.

flashcards.front: What is the source of CRSP?
flashcards.back: Local retail selling prices from manufacturers or designated dealers.

flashcards.front: What is subtracted from CRSP to move toward Customs value?
flashcards.back: Standard profit margin, depreciation, VAT, excise duty, and import duty loaded in the local price.

flashcards.front: What special point is made when reversing from residual value to Customs value?
flashcards.back: The tax calculation is done in reverse order and by subtraction.

flashcards.front: In the Hilux example, what was the brand-new CRSP?
What depreciation was used in the example?
What residual value remained after depreciation?
What Customs value was reached in the example?
If transaction value is higher than CRSP-derived value, which one is used?
flashcards.back: The higher value.

flashcards.front: What is a Customs warehouse under Section 2?
flashcards.back: A place approved for deposit of unentered, unexamined, abandoned, detained, or seized goods for securing duties.

flashcards.front: What is warehousing in situ?
flashcards.back: Treating goods as warehoused in another place ordered by the proper officer.

flashcards.front: When may warehouse rent arise?
flashcards.back: When goods remain unentered beyond 21 days or are entered but not removed within 21 days.

flashcards.front: Can the Commissioner waive warehouse rent?
flashcards.back: Yes, wholly or partly, but only in exceptional or extraordinary circumstances.

flashcards.front: How may goods in Customs warehouse be disposed of?
flashcards.back: Public auction, destruction, private treaty, or any method the Commissioner deems fit.

flashcards.front: Are the 21 days calendar days or working days?
flashcards.back: Working days; weekends and public holidays are excluded.

flashcards.front: What is the Customs Warehouse Date (CWD)?
flashcards.back: The 22nd working day from the date of discharge.

flashcards.front: What is the warehouse rent formula?
flashcards.back: CBM × number of rent days × rate per CBM × exchange rate.

flashcards.front: What simplified rate is given?
flashcards.back: $0.3 × exchange rate × CBM × number of days.

flashcards.front: Is every restricted good illegal?
flashcards.back: No. It is legal if the required conditions are met.

flashcards.front: Is every prohibited good detainable only after permit review?
flashcards.back: No. Prohibited goods are not allowed at all; permits do not legalize them unless the law specifically allows.

flashcards.front: Who carries the burden of proving correctness of the entry?
flashcards.back: The owner.

flashcards.front: Can goods be entered before the ship lands?
flashcards.back: Yes, through pre-arrival processing.

flashcards.front: Can bona fide personal baggage be cleared without entry?
flashcards.back: Yes.

flashcards.front: Are goods in a transit shed treated as already delivered?
flashcards.back: No. They are deemed still in the importing vessel or aircraft until delivery.

flashcards.front: Can a female suspect be searched by a male officer?
flashcards.back: No.

flashcards.front: If excess landed cargo is found, what regularization is used?
flashcards.back: Supplementary manifest.

flashcards.front: If cargo was manifested but is absent physically, what is that called?
flashcards.back: Short landed cargo.

flashcards.front: If cargo is present physically but absent from the manifest, what is that called?
flashcards.back: Excess landed cargo.

flashcards.front: What document authorizes coastwise carriage?
flashcards.back: Transire.

flashcards.front: What is the first tax built on Customs value in ordinary import computation?
flashcards.back: Import duty.

flashcards.front: What is the last major tax in the whiskey example?
flashcards.back: VAT.

flashcards.front: In CRSP reverse computation, do you add taxes or subtract them?
flashcards.back: Subtract them.

flashcards.front: What are the three headline roles of PGAs?
flashcards.back: Regulatory, security, and tax collection.

flashcards.front: Explain prohibited vs restricted goods in one minute.
flashcards.back: Define both, mention legal effect, give examples, and explain that restricted goods may be cleared only with agency approvals.

flashcards.front: Explain the procedure when cargo arrives overland by vehicle.
flashcards.back: Mention reporting at frontier port, declaration, documents, answering questions, and due entry under Section 29.

flashcards.front: Explain how wet cargo by pipeline is controlled.
flashcards.back: State Section 32 duties: report and record quantities/nature and provide apparatus for records.

flashcards.front: Explain rummaging in one minute.
flashcards.back: Define it, cite officer power, mention crew assistance, order of search, seizure of undeclared goods, and reporting.

flashcards.front: Explain the difference between short landed and excess landed cargo.
flashcards.back: One is manifested but missing; the other is unmanifested but present.

flashcards.front: Explain what it means for goods to be 'entered.'
flashcards.back: Proper lodgment plus payment/deposit/security as required by law.

flashcards.front: Explain why pre-arrival processing is important.
flashcards.back: It speeds up release, reduces delays, and lowers costs.

flashcards.front: Explain what Form C36 is and who completes it.
flashcards.back: It is the declaration of value completed by the owner, not the agent, depending on supplier relationship.

flashcards.front: Explain why Customs declarations are standardized.
flashcards.back: To facilitate international trade and ensure uniform minimum data through the UN layout key/SAD model.

flashcards.front: Explain the legal importance of PGAs at the border.
flashcards.back: Customs does not work alone; PGAs regulate specialized goods, security, health, standards, wildlife, biosafety, etc.

flashcards.front: Explain the main purpose of the Revised Kyoto Convention.
flashcards.back: Modern, efficient, transparent Customs procedures that both facilitate trade and protect control.

flashcards.front: Explain the logic of import tax computation in order.
flashcards.back: Get CIF in local currency, compute import duty, then excise on CIF plus duty, then VAT on CIF plus duty plus excise.

flashcards.front: Memory hook for prohibited vs restricted?
flashcards.back: Prohibited = No entry. Restricted = Entry only with conditions.

flashcards.front: Memory hook for overland arrival duties?
flashcards.back: Report, inform, declare, answer, produce, enter.

flashcards.front: Memory hook for inward report forms?
flashcards.back: C2 vessel, C4 aircraft, C3 parcels, C5 passengers, C6 stores, C7 crew.

flashcards.front: Memory hook for tax order?
flashcards.back: CIF → Duty → Excise → VAT.

flashcards.front: Memory hook for short vs excess landed?
flashcards.back: Short = on paper, not on ship. Excess = on ship, not on paper.

flashcards.front: Memory hook for C36 certificates?
flashcards.back: A = Apart from supplier. B = Bound/related to supplier.

flashcards.front: Memory hook for warehouse rent start?
flashcards.back: 21 working days free; 22nd working day starts warehouse status.

flashcards.front: Memory hook for sources of Customs law?
flashcards.back: Treaty, Protocol, Council, Court, Acts, International law.
